the literal constructs immutable List<T> and is never context-rewritten to NumericArray; without context, an empty literal is not inferable and every element is first normalized independently: unsuffixed integer to Int, unsuffixed float to Float64, and a suffixed numeric literal to exactly its suffix type; without context, only identical normalized element types are admitted; neither heterogeneous numeric join nor nominal common-supertype synthesis is permitted; with a fixed contextual List<T>, a nonnumeric element's normalized type must equal T exactly; assignment compatibility, subtype conversion, variance, hidden conversion, clone, ownership weakening, Option insertion, and source-order preference do not count as equality; integer suffixes map exactly as i8/i16/i32/i64/i128 to Int8/Int16/Int32/Int64/Int128 and u8/u16/u32/u64/u128 to UInt8/UInt16/UInt32/UInt64/UInt128; isize/usize map to ISize/USize; f32/f64 map to Float32/Float64; with a fixed contextual List<T>, an unsuffixed integer whose mathematical signed value is v may adapt to Int or to IntN when -2^(N-1) <= v <= 2^(N-1)-1, or to UIntN when 0 <= v <= 2^N-1, for N in {8,16,32,64,128}; an unsuffixed integer never implicitly adapts to ISize or USize; those targets require an isize/usize suffix or explicit checked conversion; a suffixed numeric literal must exactly match T; no widening, narrowing, signed/unsigned conversion, or integer/float conversion is implicit; an unsuffixed float normalizes only to Float64, while Float32 requires the f32 suffix and Float64 also admits the exact f64 suffix; there is no implicit integer/float, signed/unsigned, width-changing, nominal, union, or source-order-dependent join
